perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Renewal This agreement will be automatically renewed at the end of every ten (10) year term, with each subsequent term of renewal being for a ten (10) year term. A six (6) months notice must be given by either party of their intention to terminate relations due to any reason other than breach of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Renewal This agreement will only be renewed by mutual agreement of both parties at the end of every ten (10) year term. If either party wishes to renew, they must provide written notice at least six (6) months prior to the end of the current term. Failure to provide notice constitutes an immediate agreement to terminate.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that the agreement will be automatically renewed every ten years unless a six-month termination notice is given. The modified clause now states that renewal requires mutual agreement. This introduces a contradiction: one implies automatic renewal, while the other necessitates active agreement, making it uncertain whether the contract auto-renews or requires explicit consent. The modification in section 1.3 contradicts the original automatic renewal provision.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.3 Renewal This agreement will only be renewed by mutual agreement of both parties at the end of every ten (10) year term. If either party wishes to renew, they must provide written notice at least six (6) months prior to the end of the current term. Failure to provide notice constitutes an immediate agreement to terminate.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement will automatically renew, whereas the changed text says that the renewal requires mutual agreement. The contradicted text is the changed text, meaning the change that was introduced to Section 1.3 directly contradicts the old version of Section 1.3. The change from automatic renewal to requiring mutual agreement creates a direct conflict within the contract about the renewal terms, satisfying the definition of a structural flaw leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13.2 The Manufacturer grants exclusive rights to the Customer for the term of ten (10) years from the date of the signing of this agreement and for an indefinite period upon the customer fulfilling the minimum annual purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2 The Manufacturer grants exclusive rights to the Customer for the term of ten (10) years from the date of the signing of this agreement. After ten years, the rights may be revoked at any time with a 30-day notice from the manufacturer regardless if the customer is still fulfilling the minimum annual purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Customer gets exclusive rights for the initial ten years and indefinite period upon fulfilling purchase requirements. The modification permits the Manufacturer to revoke these rights after ten years regardless of the minimum annual purchase, creating a contradiction. It is now uncertain whether fulfilling the purchase requirement guarantees continued exclusive rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.2 The Manufacturer grants exclusive rights to the Customer for the term of ten (10) years from the date of the signing of this agreement. After ten years, the rights may be revoked at any time with a 30-day notice from the manufacturer regardless if the customer is still fulfilling the minimum annual purchase requirement as listed in Schedule B. of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 13.2, initially granting exclusive rights indefinitely upon fulfilling purchase requirements (Schedule B). The modified version allows the Manufacturer to revoke these rights after ten years, regardless of fulfilling purchase requirements. This directly contradicts the initial condition for continued exclusive rights, creating a clear and significant inconsistency in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product, provided that the Customer shall within seven (7) days after receipt of such alleged defective Product, notify the Manufacturer of its rejection and either: (i) request to destroy in field for credit of the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs (with approval), or (ii) request a Return Material Authorization ("RMA") number and within seven (7) days of receipt of the RMA number from the Manufacturer return such rejected Product to the Manufacturer. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer shall credit to the Customer the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product. The notification and return process for rejected products will be determined at the sole discretion of the Manufacturer on a case-by-case basis. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer may, at its sole discretion, choose to either credit the Customer the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs, or provide a replacement product in the next shipment.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear procedure for rejecting defective products. The modification introduces uncertainty by stating that the notification and return process will be determined at the sole discretion of the Manufacturer on a case-by-case basis, contradicting the established process. Also, regarding the refund, previously the manufacturer 'shall credit' implying that it is mandatory, after the change it introduces 'may, at its sole discretion, choose to either credit' implying that now it is at the discretion of the manufacturer to pay the credit or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product. The notification and return process for rejected products will be determined at the sole discretion of the Manufacturer on a case-by-case basis. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer may, at its sole discretion, choose to either credit the Customer the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs, or provide a replacement product in the next shipment.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that the notification and return process for rejected products will be determined at the sole discretion of the Manufacturer on a case-by-case basis. This directly contradicts the earlier, more specific procedure outlined in the original Section 5.3. Furthermore, the original text mandates that 'the Manufacturer shall credit to the Customer', while the changed text states that the Manufacturer 'may, at its sole discretion, choose to either credit'. This introduces uncertainty and gives the Manufacturer discretionary power, conflicting with the original mandatory obligation to provide a credit. This satisfies the condition of Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction, making the contradiction strong.
